text: this is a video demonstration of robotic posterior exentration for locally advanced rectal cancer using davinci xi platform the patient is a fifty six year old postmenopausal lady with a mid rectal tumor starting six centimeters from inner verge and infiltrating posterior vaginal wall and cervix she has received neo adjuvant chemo radiation and now plan for posterior exentration four robotic ports are inserted along a straight line which is four centimeters superior and parallel to the line joining right anterior superior iliac spine and umbilicus

The surgical team prepares for robotic posterior exenteration using the Da Vinci Xi platform. Four robotic ports are inserted along a straight line, positioned four centimeters superior and parallel to the line joining the right anterior superior iliac spine and the umbilicus.
The surgeon performs the abdominal dissection using robotic instruments, following the technique of a standard robotic total mesorectal excision.
The surgeon performs the abdominal dissection using robotic instruments, following the standard technique for total mesorectal excision.
The surgeon creates a dissection plane beneath the inferior mesenteric vein, extending it laterally to separate the vein from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon creates a dissection plane underneath the inferior mesenteric vein, extending the dissection laterally.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues using robotic instruments.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube, progressing the dissection from the midline towards the lateral aspect of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon dissects and divides the inferior mesenteric artery beyond the origin of the left colic artery, using robotic instruments to carefully separate and cut the vessel.
The surgeon dissects and divides the inferior mesenteric artery beyond the origin of the left colic artery, using robotic instruments to carefully separate and transect the vessel.
The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon and splenic flexure, separating these structures from surrounding tissues using robotic instruments.

The surgeon performs posterior rectal mobilization, advancing the dissection down to the pelvic floor, ensuring the rectum is freed from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon initiates lateral dissection by addressing the infundibulopelvic ligaments, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon divides the peritoneum along the line of TALT, a visible step in the rectopexy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and lifts the infundibulopelvic ligament away from the underlying retroperitoneal tissue, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.

The surgeon divides the posterior leaf of the broad ligament to isolate the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon divides the posterior leaf of the broad ligament to isolate the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the dissection.
The surgeon clips the infundibulopelvic ligament and then divides it, completing the dissection of this structure.
The surgeon clips the infundibulopelvic ligament and then divides it, completing the lateral dissection.
Following the division of the infundibulopelvic ligament, the surgeon fully opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament and mobilizes the parametral tissue medially to establish clear margins.

The surgeon performs medial mobilization of the ureter, facilitating the dissection of the uterine vessels at their root. The ureter is carefully maneuvered to ensure clear access to the vessels.

The surgeon traces the obliterated umbilical artery down to its origin by dividing the overlying soft tissue, revealing the artery's origin.

The surgeon performs a gentle dissection distal to the previously divided structure, revealing the origin of the uterine vessels.
The surgeon performs a gentle dissection distal to the previously divided infernibulopelvic ligament, revealing the origin of the uterine vessels.
The surgeon dissects, clips, and individually divides the uterine artery and uterine vein, ensuring clear separation of these vascular structures.
The surgeon individually dissects, clips, and divides both the uterine artery and uterine vein, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon repeats the dissection and division of the uterine artery and vein on the right side, following the same steps as previously performed on the left.

The surgeon divides the anterior peritoneum to mobilize the bladder, carefully separating it from the cervix and upper part of the vagina.
The surgeon divides the anterior peritoneum, mobilizing the bladder away from the cervix and upper part of the vagina, creating a clear separation between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon observes the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, noting its short length due to the patient's postmenopausal status.
The surgeon observes that the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is very short, likely due to the patient's postmenopausal status.

The surgeon performs an anterior colpotomy after mobilizing the anterior vaginal wall, extending the incision laterally. Before completing the lateral cuts, cauterization is applied to the soft tissue beyond the vaginal walls to manage the descending uterine vessels and vaginal arteries.
The surgeon performs an anterior colpotomy after adequately mobilizing the anterior vaginal wall, extending the incision laterally.

The surgeon divides the posterior vaginal wall and dissects it away from the anterior wall of the rectum, ensuring safe closure of the vaginal vault and adequate mobilization of the distal rectal stump for colorectal anastomosis.
